feed.title: Combined summary - Babilonia - probabilistic coinjoin and covert betting

entry.title: Combined summary - Babilonia - probabilistic coinjoin and covert betting
entry.summary: A new cryptocurrency protocol has been developed, focusing primarily on enhancing privacy and efficiency through a minimal onchain footprint. This protocol only requires two payment-size transactions for operation, typically needing one confirmation but occasionally a third. It leverages simple Zero-Knowledge Proof (ZKP) techniques, ensuring that the transactions remain indistinguishable from typical payments or payjoins, thus supporting invisible and fair betting between two participants. The protocol introduces the concept of "pot sweeteners," which allows incentives to be added to transactions without leaving traceable marks, differentiating it from other methods like those in Joinmarket. Another key feature is the use of decoys in BIP324 traffic, which facilitates negotiation between two nodes without visibility, enhancing protection against Sybil attacks. The protocol's source code, mainly authored using LLM coding with minimal human input, can be reviewed and tested via its [GitHub repository](https://github.com/AdamISZ/babilonia). Technical insights reveal the protocol's potential applications in swaps, though with some tradeoffs. The full capabilities and theoretical underpinnings are further discussed in the [Babilonia paper](https://github.com/AdamISZ/babilonia-paper), and example transactions demonstrating these capabilities are available on the Signet test network [here](https://mempool.space/signet/tx/59bb7633314b35d2edcff7997281c0254e9a9b36faf22dbc643d4a319d92f064) and [here](https://mempool.space/signet/tx/1112032da666df1efaae5c2e8d567bfa200e0a94dd1c5aa8e1e89a14b32f4c44). Comparatively, the discussion also encompasses GTT 26, another cryptographic protocol using an Oblivious Pseudo-Random Function (OPRF) for transactions, which supports complex outcomes suitable for lotteries. Unlike Babilonia, GTT 26 requires interaction with the dealer’s secret key and is not as suited for simpler transaction types like coinjoin or payjoin due to its complex structure. Babilonia offers a more streamlined approach particularly beneficial for coinswap applications, where it suggests a novel method to mitigate amount correlation issues through privately negotiated variable transaction fees. This method not only enhances privacy but also maintains fairness, unlike traditional fixed-fee structures that do little to obscure transaction correlations. In operational terms, a developer named Claude has provided a simplified explanation of Babilonia aimed at Bitcoin engineers, accessible [here](https://gist.github.com/AdamISZ/ebae2d5f547f56c6f8164db19f718795). This resource outlines practical implications and technical details crucial for real-world implementation of such cryptographic solutions. In summary, while GTT 26 is ideal for complex systems requiring high security, Babilonia offers a practical, privacy-focused solution for everyday cryptocurrency transactions.
